prompt: At its core, what does an ideal diode do in a circuit?
choices: Acts as a one-way valve: current flows freely in one direction and is blocked in the other
Limits the current to a fixed safe value in both directions
Drops exactly half the supply voltage regardless of orientation
Stores charge on one side and releases it on the other
explanation: A diode is a check valve for charge. Push current from anode to cathode and it looks like a closed switch; try to push it the other way and it looks like an open circuit. It does not regulate current magnitude (that's a resistor's or regulator's job), and it stores essentially nothing — that's a capacitor. Everything else about diodes (rectifiers, clippers, LED drivers) is this one idea applied.

prompt: Is $D_1$ conducting in this circuit?
choices: Yes — the source pushes current into the anode, so the diode is forward biased
No — the diode blocks all DC current
No — the resistor prevents the diode from reaching its turn-on voltage
Only during half of each cycle
explanation: Trace the loop: current leaves the source's + terminal (top), flows right into $D_1$'s anode, and out its cathode toward the resistor. That is the diode's easy direction — the triangle points the way conventional current flows — so it conducts. The resistor doesn't stop turn-on; it just sets how much current flows once the diode's $\approx 0.7\,\text{V}$ is paid. And this is a DC source, so 'half of each cycle' doesn't apply.

prompt: Is $D_1$ conducting in this circuit?
choices: No — the source pushes current against the diode's arrow, so it is reverse biased and blocks
Yes — a 5 V source is more than enough to force any diode to conduct
Yes — but only 0.7 V worth of current flows
No — because the resistor limits the current to zero
explanation: Compared to the previous idea, the diode is flipped: the source's + terminal now drives current into the *cathode* side. A reverse-biased diode is an open switch, so essentially zero current flows and the full 5 V appears across the diode (the resistor drops nothing, since $V = IR = 0$). Supply size doesn't matter — short of breakdown, a reverse-biased diode blocks 5 V as easily as 0.5 V. '0.7 V worth of current' isn't a thing: 0.7 V is a voltage drop, not a current.

prompt: The LED drops $2\,\text{V}$ when lit, and you want $10\,\text{mA}$ through it. What value should $R_1$ be, in $\Omega$?
explanation: The LED claims its 2 V first; the resistor gets whatever is left over, and that leftover is what Ohm's law applies to. Left over: $5 - 2 = 3\,\text{V}$. Then $R = V/I = 3 / 0.010 = 300\,\Omega$. The classic mistake is dividing the full 5 V by 10 mA (giving 500 Ω) — always subtract the diode drop before reaching for Ohm's law.

prompt: Using the constant-drop model ($V_D = 0.7\,\text{V}$), find the current through $R_1$, in mA.
explanation: The diode is forward biased (anode toward the + terminal), so treat it as a 0.7 V toll booth: it conducts, but charges 0.7 V for passage. The resistor sees the rest: $9 - 0.7 = 8.3\,\text{V}$, so $I = 8.3 / 1\text{k} = 8.3\,\text{mA}$. One loop, one current — that same 8.3 mA flows through the diode too.

prompt: The dashed curve is the input, the solid curve is the output. What circuit sits between them?
choices: A half-wave rectifier — a single series diode that passes positive half-cycles and blocks negative ones
A full-wave rectifier — it flips negative half-cycles upward
A voltage divider — it scales the sine down
A clamper — it shifts the whole waveform up by its peak value
explanation: The output copies the input whenever the input is positive and sits at zero whenever it's negative — that's the one-way valve doing its thing. A single series diode conducts on positive half-cycles and opens on negative ones, so half of every cycle is simply thrown away. A full-wave rectifier would *fold* the negative lobes upward instead of deleting them; a divider would shrink the sine but keep both halves; a clamper preserves the shape and only shifts its DC level.

prompt: A full-wave rectifier is fed a $50\,\text{Hz}$ sine; the plot shows its output. What is the fundamental frequency of the output ripple?
choices: $100\,\text{Hz}$ — folding the negative half-cycles up doubles the number of humps per second
$50\,\text{Hz}$ — rectification doesn't change frequency
$25\,\text{Hz}$ — rectification halves the frequency
$0\,\text{Hz}$ — the output is pure DC
explanation: Full-wave rectification takes $|v|$: every negative lobe is flipped positive, so one input cycle (20 ms at 50 Hz) produces *two* identical humps. Read it off the plot — the pattern repeats every 10 ms, which is 100 Hz. This doubling is a gift for power supplies: ripple at twice the frequency is twice as easy for the same filter capacitor to smooth.

prompt: With no load connected, find the current through the zener diode, in mA. The zener regulates at $V_Z = 5.1\,\text{V}$.
explanation: A zener used as a regulator sits in *reverse* breakdown on purpose: it pins its node at $V_Z$ and swallows whatever current the resistor delivers. The resistor sees the difference between supply and zener voltage: $12 - 5.1 = 6.9\,\text{V}$, so $I = 6.9 / 470 \approx 14.7\,\text{mA}$. With no load, every bit of that current goes through the zener — the price of regulation is burning current even when nothing is connected.

prompt: The zener holds $V_{out}$ at $V_Z = 6.2\,\text{V}$. Find the current through the zener itself, in mA.
explanation: Think of the zener as taking the leftovers: the resistor delivers a fixed total current, the load takes what it needs, and the zener absorbs the rest to hold the voltage. Total in: $I_R = (12 - 6.2)/220 \approx 26.4\,\text{mA}$. Load takes: $I_L = 6.2/1\text{k} = 6.2\,\text{mA}$. Zener gets: $26.4 - 6.2 \approx 20.2\,\text{mA}$. This bookkeeping (KCL at the output node) is also how you find the maximum load a zener regulator can support — when the load wants all 26.4 mA, the zener starves and regulation fails.

prompt: The LED drops $2\,\text{V}$ when lit. Set $R_1$ so the LED current is $20\,\text{mA}$.
explanation: The LED takes its 2 V off the top no matter what; the resistor's job is to convert the remaining $9 - 2 = 7\,\text{V}$ into the current you want. $R = 7 / 0.020 = 350\,\Omega$. Notice the lever you're pulling: smaller $R$ means more current and a brighter (or eventually dead) LED — the resistor is the only thing standing between the LED and the supply.

prompt: Why does an LED need a series resistor at all? Why not just connect it straight across a supply slightly above its forward voltage?
choices: The diode's I–V curve is exponential — a tiny voltage excess causes a huge, poorly controlled current, so something else must set the current
Without a resistor the LED cannot reach its turn-on voltage
The resistor raises the voltage to the level the LED needs
LEDs only work with pulsed current, and the resistor provides the pulses
explanation: An LED is a voltage cliff, not a voltage knob: past its forward voltage, current grows exponentially, roughly 10× for every extra 60 mV. Supply tolerance, temperature drift, and part-to-part variation make 'slightly above' impossible to hold, so the current would be anywhere from dim to destructive. The series resistor takes over as the current-setter: $I = (V_{supply} - V_{LED})/R$ is dominated by $R$, which is stable. Diodes set voltage; resistors set current — pair them accordingly.

prompt: Two silicon diodes ($0.7\,\text{V}$ each, constant-drop model) are in series with a $330\,\Omega$ resistor across a $5\,\text{V}$ supply, oriented to conduct. Find the circuit current, in mA.
explanation: Diode drops in series simply stack — each toll booth charges its own 0.7 V. The resistor is left with $5 - 0.7 - 0.7 = 3.6\,\text{V}$, so $I = 3.6 / 330 \approx 10.9\,\text{mA}$. Stacked diode drops are a real design tool: two or three in series make a crude low-voltage reference or a level shifter.

prompt: A $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ sine drives this circuit. What does $V_{out}$ look like? (Use the $0.7\,\text{V}$ drop model.)
choices: The negative half-cycles pass through, but positive excursions are clipped at about $+0.7\,\text{V}$
The positive half-cycles pass through, but negative excursions are clipped at about $-0.7\,\text{V}$
A full sine shifted down so its peak sits at $0.7\,\text{V}$
Nothing — the diode shorts the output to ground at all times
explanation: This is a clipper: the diode sits *across* the output, ready to short it to ground — but only in its conducting direction. Its anode is at $V_{out}$, so it turns on whenever $V_{out}$ tries to exceed $+0.7\,\text{V}$, clamping the node there while $R_1$ absorbs the excess. On negative swings the diode is reverse biased, effectively absent, and the input passes through untouched. A shunt diode clips; the resistor in front is what makes clipping possible without shorting the source.

prompt: A clamper (series capacitor into the signal path, diode from the output node to ground) and a clipper (series resistor, diode to ground) are both single-diode circuits. What is the essential difference in what they do to a sine wave?
choices: A clamper shifts the whole waveform's DC level while preserving its shape; a clipper flattens off part of the waveform and destroys that portion
A clamper halves the amplitude; a clipper doubles it
They do the same thing — the capacitor only filters noise
A clipper works on AC only, while a clamper works on DC only
explanation: The capacitor is the tell. In a clamper, the diode conducts briefly to charge the capacitor to (roughly) the signal's peak, and thereafter that stored voltage adds in series with the input — the entire sine rides up or down to a new DC level, shape intact, with the diode barely conducting again. In a clipper there's nothing to store: whenever the signal crosses the diode's threshold, the diode conducts and *holds* the output there, amputating that part of the waveform. Shift versus chop — remember 'clamper = DC restorer'.

prompt: The plot shows a silicon diode's forward I–V curve. What is the practical takeaway?
choices: Current is negligible below about $0.6\,\text{V}$, then rises so steeply that the voltage stays pinned near $0.7\,\text{V}$ — which is exactly why the constant-drop model works
The diode behaves like a resistor of constant slope over its whole range
Current is proportional to the square of the voltage
The diode conducts equally well at any forward voltage
explanation: Read the curve: essentially flat-zero until $\sim 0.6\,\text{V}$, then nearly vertical. The real relationship is exponential ($I \approx I_S e^{V/nV_T}$), so current multiplies by ~10 for every extra 60 mV — huge current changes barely move the voltage. That's why we can cheat and say 'a conducting silicon diode drops 0.7 V, period': the wall is so steep that the operating point can't be anywhere else. A resistor would be a straight line through the origin; this is anything but.

prompt: A half-wave rectifier uses one silicon diode ($0.7\,\text{V}$ drop) between a sine source with $10\,\text{V}$ peak and a resistive load. What is the peak output voltage across the load, in volts?
explanation: The diode charges its 0.7 V toll on the way through, so the load never sees the full input peak. At the instant the input crests at 10 V, the output crests at $10 - 0.7 = 9.3\,\text{V}$. Small detail, real consequence: for small signals (say a 1 V peak sine) that 0.7 V toll eats most of the waveform, which is why precision rectifiers wrap the diode in an op-amp.

prompt: A full-wave bridge rectifier ($0.7\,\text{V}$ per diode) is driven by a sine with $12\,\text{V}$ peak. On each half-cycle the current path passes through two diodes. Find the peak output voltage, in volts.
explanation: In a bridge, current always threads through *two* diodes in series — one into the load, one back out — so the toll doubles: $2 \times 0.7 = 1.4\,\text{V}$. Peak output: $12 - 1.4 = 10.6\,\text{V}$. This is the bridge's tradeoff versus a center-tapped full-wave design (one diode drop, but a more expensive transformer): you pay an extra 0.7 V for skipping the center tap, which matters a lot in low-voltage supplies.

prompt: A rectifier feeds a filter capacitor that supplies a load. The output shows a sawtooth 'ripple'. Which change reduces the ripple?
choices: A larger filter capacitor — it stores more charge, so its voltage sags less between rectifier peaks
A smaller filter capacitor — it charges faster
A heavier load (more current drawn) — it stabilizes the capacitor
Removing the capacitor entirely
explanation: Between rectifier peaks the diodes are off and the capacitor alone carries the load, draining like a water tank between refills. Ripple is how far the tank level falls per cycle: a bigger tank (larger $C$) sags less for the same draw, and a thirstier load ($\uparrow I$) drains it faster, making ripple *worse*. Full-wave rectification also helps — refills come twice as often. That's the whole ripple story: $V_r \approx I/(fC)$.

prompt: A full-wave rectifier (ripple frequency $f = 120\,\text{Hz}$) feeds a $1000\,\mu\text{F}$ filter capacitor and a load drawing a steady $50\,\text{mA}$. Using the approximation $V_r \approx I/(fC)$, find the peak-to-peak ripple, in volts.
explanation: The capacitor loses charge $Q = I \cdot \Delta t$ during each discharge interval, and the voltage sag is that charge divided by $C$. With refills every $1/120\,\text{s}$: $V_r \approx \frac{I}{fC} = \frac{0.050}{120 \times 0.001} \approx 0.42\,\text{V}$. Feel the knobs in the formula: double the capacitance or double the ripple frequency (full-wave vs half-wave) and ripple halves; double the load current and it doubles.

prompt: Two supplies drive the same output node through diodes: $5\,\text{V}$ through $D_1$ and $3.3\,\text{V}$ through $D_2$ (anodes at the supplies, cathodes joined at the node), with a load resistor from the node to ground. Using $0.7\,\text{V}$ drops, what happens?
choices: $V_{out} = 4.3\,\text{V}$; $D_2$ is reverse biased and the 3.3 V supply delivers no current
$V_{out} = 2.6\,\text{V}$; only the lower supply wins because its diode turns on first
$V_{out} = 6.9\,\text{V}$; the two supplies add through their diodes
Both diodes conduct equally and $V_{out} = 3.45\,\text{V}$, the average
explanation: This is diode OR-ing: each supply *offers* the node its voltage minus a diode drop, and the highest offer wins. $D_1$ pulls the node up to $5 - 0.7 = 4.3\,\text{V}$. Now check $D_2$: its anode sits at 3.3 V but its cathode sits at 4.3 V — reverse biased, so it politely disconnects its supply. This is exactly how battery-backup circuits work: the backup diode sits reverse biased until the main supply sags below it, then takes over automatically.

prompt: Two supplies feed one load through diodes — a diode-OR. **Click the diode that is conducting.**
explanation: Each supply *offers* the shared node its voltage minus one diode drop, and the highest offer wins. $V_1$ pulls the node up to $12 - 0.7 \approx 11.3\,\text{V}$ through $D_1$ (the 1 Ω internal resistance drops only millivolts at this current). Now look at $D_2$: its anode sits at 9 V but its cathode sits at 11.3 V — reverse biased, so the 9 V supply is politely disconnected. One node, many diodes: only the winner conducts; every other diode is held off by the very voltage the winner establishes.

prompt: The battery has been inserted **backwards** (+ terminal at the bottom — check the figure). What happens to the load $R_L$?
choices: Nothing — $D_1$ is now reverse biased and blocks all current, so the load never sees the reversed voltage
The load sees $-8.3\,\text{V}$ — the diode conducts and only subtracts its 0.7 V
The full $-9\,\text{V}$ appears across the load and may damage it
The diode conducts a huge current and burns out
explanation: This is reverse-polarity protection: the diode is a one-way valve placed so that only *correct* battery polarity can push current through it. Backwards, the battery drives current into $D_1$'s cathode — the blocking direction — so zero current flows and the load is untouched; the diode itself absorbs the full 9 V of reverse bias, which it shrugs off. The price you pay is 0.7 V lost during *normal* operation, which is why designs that can't spare it use a Schottky diode or a MOSFET-based 'ideal diode' instead.

prompt: A series protection diode ($0.7\,\text{V}$ drop) is no big deal in a $12\,\text{V}$ car circuit but a real problem on a $3.3\,\text{V}$ board. Why?
choices: Because the toll is fixed but the budget isn't: $0.7\,\text{V}$ is over a fifth of $3.3\,\text{V}$, leaving only $2.6\,\text{V}$ — possibly below what the chips are guaranteed to run at
Because diodes cannot conduct when the supply is below about 5 V
Because a diode's forward drop grows as the supply voltage shrinks
It isn't a problem — the 0.7 V drop scales down in proportion to the supply
explanation: A diode charges the same absolute toll no matter how rich the circuit is: 0.7 V is under 6% of a 12 V supply but 21% of a 3.3 V one. After the diode, the 3.3 V board runs on 2.6 V, which can sit below the minimum operating voltage of its chips — and every milliamp the board draws burns $0.7\,\text{V} \times I$ in the diode as pure heat. Low-voltage designs therefore reach for Schottky diodes ($\approx 0.3\,\text{V}$) or MOSFET 'ideal diode' circuits that drop only millivolts.

prompt: Two LEDs, two resistors, one supply — but the LEDs point opposite ways. **Click the LED that actually lights up.**
explanation: An LED is a diode first and a light source second: it only emits when current flows anode → cathode. $D_1$'s anode faces the + rail, so current flows down through it — about $(5-2)/330 \approx 9\,\text{mA}$ — and it lights. $D_2$ is flipped: the supply pushes into its cathode, it blocks, and it stays completely dark (a reverse-biased LED emits nothing at all). This is why an LED wired backwards simply doesn't work — and why LEDs make handy free polarity indicators.

prompt: $D_1$ is a flyback (freewheeling) diode meant to protect the switch from the relay coil's inductive kick when the switch opens. Is $D_1$ oriented correctly?
choices: Yes — with its cathode toward the + rail it sits idle during normal operation, but the instant the switch opens it gives the coil current a safe loop to keep circulating in
No — it should be flipped so it conducts while the switch is closed and absorbs energy continuously
No — a flyback diode belongs in series with the coil, not across it
Yes, but only because the diode also carries the coil's normal operating current
explanation: An inductor's current cannot stop instantly — open the switch and the coil will generate whatever voltage it takes to keep its current flowing, arcing across the switch contacts if you let it. The fix is to give that current somewhere to go: $D_1$ points 'up' so the coil current can circulate coil → diode → coil, decaying gently while the diode clamps the kick to one diode drop. During normal operation the diode's cathode sits at +12 V, so it's reverse biased and does nothing. Flipped, it would short the supply through the switch — the orientation is everything.

prompt: You can model a diode as an ideal switch ($0\,\text{V}$ drop), as a constant $0.7\,\text{V}$ drop, or with the full exponential equation. What's the right way to think about these three models?
choices: They're an accuracy ladder: ideal for figuring out *which* diodes conduct, 0.7 V for everyday hand design, exponential when precision demands it — use the cheapest model that answers your question
The exponential model is the physically correct one, so the other two should never be used
They describe different diode types: some diodes are ideal, some drop 0.7 V, some are exponential
The models differ only in how much current the diode can safely carry
explanation: All three describe the *same* diode at different zoom levels. The ideal model answers topology questions — which way does current flow, which diodes are on — in your head. The 0.7 V model adds the one correction that usually matters and powers nearly all hand calculation (as in this app's simulator). The exponential law is the truth, but you only need it for precision work, temperature analysis, or small-signal behavior. Engineering skill isn't using the fanciest model — it's knowing when the cheap one is good enough.

prompt: Zener diodes are deliberately operated in *reverse* — the direction where ordinary diodes block. Why on earth would you use a diode backwards?
choices: Because reverse breakdown happens at a sharp, precisely manufactured voltage $V_Z$, and sitting on that cliff turns the diode into a stable voltage reference — forward, it's just another 0.7 V diode
Because zeners physically cannot conduct in the forward direction
Because reverse operation makes the diode switch faster
Because running them backwards keeps them from overheating
explanation: Every diode breaks down in reverse eventually; a zener is one where that breakdown voltage is engineered to be precise, repeatable, and non-destructive. In breakdown the I–V curve is nearly vertical — current can swing wildly while the voltage barely moves — which is exactly the property you want in a voltage reference or crude regulator. Forward biased, a zener behaves like any silicon diode ($\approx 0.7\,\text{V}$), so nothing special is gained. 'Backwards on purpose' is the whole trick: the useful terrain is the reverse cliff at $V_Z$.

prompt: Every MOSFET contains a parasitic **body diode** between source and drain (for an n-channel part: anode at the source, cathode at the drain). What is the practical consequence?
choices: An 'off' MOSFET blocks current in only one direction — the body diode conducts the other way — so a single MOSFET can never stop reverse current on its own
The body diode means MOSFETs cannot switch DC at all
The body diode only exists in p-channel devices
It acts as a second transistor that amplifies the gate signal
explanation: The body diode isn't a design choice — it falls out of the silicon structure, a pn junction you get whether you want it or not. Turn an n-MOSFET fully off and it still conducts whenever current tries to flow source → drain, because that's the body diode's forward direction. Circuits exploit this (MOSFET reverse-polarity protection deliberately orients the body diode to conduct on correct polarity) or fight it (bidirectional switches use two MOSFETs back-to-back so the body diodes oppose each other). Remember: buying a transistor gets you a free diode, like it or not.

prompt: A full-wave bridge rectifier contains four diodes. At any instant while it is delivering current, how many of them are conducting?
choices: Two — one diagonal pair steers current through the load, and the opposite pair takes over when the input reverses
One — diodes take turns individually
All four — that's what makes it 'full-wave'
Zero — the bridge couples the signal capacitively
explanation: The bridge's job is to route current through the load in the *same* direction no matter which way the source is pushing. Each polarity of the input gets its own diagonal pair: one diode carries current into the load's + terminal, and its partner carries it back out to the source — in one, back out, always two in the path. The other pair sits reverse biased, waiting for the input to flip. That two-diode toll is also why a bridge loses $2 \times 0.7 = 1.4\,\text{V}$ rather than one drop.

prompt: A diode's forward voltage *falls* about $2\,\text{mV}$ for every °C it heats up. Why does this make connecting a bare diode (or LED) straight across a stiff voltage source dangerous?
choices: It closes a vicious loop: heating lowers $V_F$, so at a fixed applied voltage the current rises, which dissipates more heat, which lowers $V_F$ further — thermal runaway
Falling $V_F$ makes the diode gradually stop conducting as it warms up
It isn't dangerous — a lower voltage drop means lower power dissipation
The temperature rise reverses the diode's polarity
explanation: The danger is positive feedback. On the diode's near-vertical I–V curve, a mere 2 mV shift moves the current a lot — so warming lowers the turn-on voltage, the stiff source drives more current through it, that current makes more heat, and around it goes until something burns. A series resistor breaks the loop with negative feedback: any rise in current increases the resistor's drop, which steals voltage *away* from the diode and pushes the current back down. This is the deeper reason current limiting is non-negotiable — not just to pick a brightness, but to make the operating point stable at all.

prompt: A main $12\,\text{V}$ supply and a $9\,\text{V}$ backup battery each feed the same load through their own silicon diode ($0.7\,\text{V}$ drop, cathodes joined at the load). As the main supply sags, at what main-supply voltage does the backup battery start to take over, in volts?
explanation: In a diode-OR, each source offers the load node its voltage minus one diode drop, and the highest offer wins. The backup's standing offer is $9 - 0.7 = 8.3\,\text{V}$; the main supply wins as long as $V_{main} - 0.7 > 8.3$, i.e. while $V_{main} > 9\,\text{V}$. Because both diodes charge the same 0.7 V toll, the drops cancel and the handover happens simply when the raw voltages cross. The switchover is automatic, instant, and needs no controller — that's why diode-ORs guard everything from RAM backup batteries to server power feeds.

prompt: This $3.3\,\text{V}$ rail reaches its load through a protection diode ($0.7\,\text{V}$ drop). **Click the node** that sits at $2.6\,\text{V}$.
explanation: Voltage drops happen *across* components, so walk the loop and collect the tolls: the supply's + terminal sits at 3.3 V, and crossing the conducting diode costs 0.7 V, leaving the far side — the diode's cathode, and everything wired to it up to $R_L$ — at $3.3 - 0.7 = 2.6\,\text{V}$. The load then drops the full 2.6 V to ground, drawing 26 mA. Notice what protection cost you: the load lives on 2.6 V, a fifth of the supply gone, which is why low-voltage designs use Schottky diodes or MOSFET ideal-diode circuits instead.

prompt: A half-wave rectifier (one diode in series with a resistive load, no filter capacitor) is driven by a sine with $10\,\text{V}$ peak. What maximum **reverse** voltage must the diode withstand?
choices: $10\,\text{V}$ — at the negative peak the diode is off, no current flows, the resistor drops nothing, so the entire source voltage lands across the diode
$0.7\,\text{V}$ — a diode never sees more than its forward drop
$20\,\text{V}$ — the reverse voltage is always twice the peak
$9.3\,\text{V}$ — the peak minus one diode drop
explanation: When the diode is off it becomes the *only* place the source voltage can appear: zero current means the resistor drops $IR = 0$, so KVL hands the whole input to the diode. At the negative crest that's the full 10 V of reverse bias. This number is the diode's **peak inverse voltage (PIV)** requirement — a rating you must check just like current. And beware: add a filter capacitor and the requirement nearly doubles, because the charged cap holds the cathode high while the input swings low.

prompt: A half-wave rectifier (silicon diode, $0.7\,\text{V}$ drop) charges a large filter capacitor from a sine with $10\,\text{V}$ peak. The load is light, so the capacitor holds its peak charge between cycles. What peak reverse voltage must the diode withstand, in volts?
explanation: The capacitor is a memory: it charges to the peak minus one diode drop, $10 - 0.7 = 9.3\,\text{V}$, and — lightly loaded — just sits there holding the diode's cathode at 9.3 V. Half a cycle later the input drives the anode down to $-10\,\text{V}$. The diode is now stretched between the two: $9.3 - (-10) = 19.3\,\text{V}$ of reverse bias, nearly *twice* the peak you might naively expect. This is why rectifier diodes in capacitor-filtered supplies are specified with PIV ratings of at least $2V_{peak}$, with margin on top.

prompt: The switch has been closed a long time, so a steady current flows in the relay coil ($L_1$ + its winding resistance $R_1$). The instant the switch opens, the coil current keeps flowing through the flyback diode $D_1$ ($0.7\,\text{V}$ drop). What peak voltage appears across the now-open switch, in volts?
explanation: Follow the current after the switch opens: the coil insists on keeping its $12/120 = 100\,\text{mA}$ flowing, and its only path is down through $R_1$, along the ground rail, and *up* through $D_1$ back to the coil's top node. A conducting $D_1$ pins that top node one diode drop **below** ground: $-0.7\,\text{V}$. The switch is now stretched between the +12 V rail on one side and $-0.7\,\text{V}$ on the other — just $12.7\,\text{V}$ across its contacts. Without the diode, the coil would slew that node hundreds of volts negative and arc the contacts; the flyback diode caps the whole drama at one diode drop.
